sentences.text: --teaches in the rec center.
And-- yeah.
And we [INAUDIBLE] our house is [INAUDIBLE].
Every year-- you don't see it so much.
But every year we-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
[INAUDIBLE].
All right.
I'm going to work.
Yeah?
Well, she's got to eat.
Yeah.
Well, if she's as quiet as possible.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Maybe the woman can help, you know, make sure that she doesn't walk in by accident or something.
Yeah.
Maybe we can close these--
We can close those.
OK, slide.
Good morning.
Susan Brunn.
B-R-U-N-N.
Susan Mendel.
M-E-N-D-E-L.
In Berlin.
In Germany.
February 22, 1923.
I'm 73 years old, for another month.
Ernst.
Helen.
My father was an actor.
No.
He wasn't.
And I-- I don't know.
He was not in Berlin, I think.
He comes from a town called Szczecin.
But I really don't know where he worked, or what-- how long he worked.
He was very young when he died.
I think 28 or 29.
He was an officer in the German army, in the First World War, and sustained some wounds, which never healed properly.
And he died on the effect of those wounds, in 1923.
10 months.
Well she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what.
What do you mean by what happened?
She lived.
And she was, of course, devastated, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had to go on with her life, and had to work.
I'm not sure about the first few years.
I was too-- too little.
I don't know.
But she used to work at all kind of things.
She was mainly a sales lady in a dry goods stores.
And then she opened her own store.
Also dry goods.
Ladies' underwear and linens.
It was all combined in Europe.
[GERMAN] Elegant.
Not really.
I-- it was-- I think it was Muller Strasse.
I'm not sure.
Oh, she made a good living.
I was-- for the first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
When I was maybe one or two, I went to live with my aunt and uncle in Raciborz, in Silesia.
And when I was nine or 10-- I-- I don't know exactly how old I was.
When my mother got me back to Berlin, she put me in a in a home in-- about an hour out of Berlin, where she could come and visit me Sundays.
And I was there until I was about 13.
And then she took me home.
Oh, that was wonderful, whatever I remember.
They were wealthy.
They had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a beautiful home.
They had a big cigar factory, which came from my grandfather, actually.
And I had a very good life.
Felix and Maria Froehlich.
Felix was my mother's brother.
They had no children.
That's why they-- they would have liked to keep me always, but it didn't work out.
I very recall-- very, very little recollection about that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just know that a very embarrassing moment was when I had my first day of school.
I was driven to school with a chauffeur.
And I felt at that time very embarrassed.
And I know that they had a car, one or two cars, at all times.
And I was driven around.
And I had a very, very comfortable, nice life.
I had a nanny who took care of me.
I remember certain things.
But I remember some stupid things.
[LAUGHS] I remember that they went on a trip for a day or two to Czechoslovakia, which was very near.
And I was used to go with them, and I wanted to go with them.
But they put me to a cousin-- I think to a cousin next door, or a couple of doors away.
And we had to play together and stay together.
And I went on the balcony, and I wanted to throw myself down because they didn't take me along.
This I see in front of me.
It's funny that something stick with you all your life.
Jewish.
They were Jews.
My aunt was not Jewish.
My aunt was Catholic.
But she was a better Jew than many Jews themselves.
I don't remember anything about that.
I just know I went to school there.
Regular--
Pardon me?
No.
No.
They celebrated every holiday, a Jewish one and a non-Jewish one.
I don't have any recollection.
I just know that there was another brother living in Raciborz, which was a younger brother of my uncle Felix.
And they were extremely Jewish.
And they were Zionists.
And all the Jewish holidays were celebrated in their house.
And I remember when we were there, each of us had a menorah, and had on Hanukkah to light the candles.
And there were two children, my cousin Maya, and my cousin Erwin.
They live now in Israel.
I think my aunt did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they never had a tree or anything like that.
When I went to the home, or when I went with-- to live with her?
Both.
Well, the home I didn't like.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- well, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I disliked living there.
I-- I don't-- I didn't like it.
And when I came home to my mother, I was happier.
And-- but I don't really remember much how it was.
I just know that we always stuck together then.
And we lived together from then on, always, all the time.
Even in my-- part of my married life, in my first marriage.
And it was nothing extraordinary.
It was just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living.
I don't know.
I don't remember very much, probably because it wasn't so very interesting or whatever.
I don't know.
It was a Jewish-- from some kind of a Jewish organization.
And it had either children there who had only one parent, or no parents at all.
Anything.
[LAUGHS] The first few months were bearable, because there were very nice people who were in charge.
And I remember even their name.
We called them Tante-- Tante Ida and Uncle Arnie.
They ran the place.
And after a short while, they either immigrated or left.
I don't know.
And there came a woman who ran the place, a very Polish, elderly lady.
I see her in front of me, but I don't know her name.
I must have been about eight or nine years old.
I'm not sure.
Nine?
Oh, yes.
In to a regular public school.
Oh, German.
We started learning in school, English and French.
A very good one.
I was so glad to be home again.
Also to public school.
Never into a private school, if-- if that's what you mean.
[SIGHS] From what I hear from all people, and what I could see, as little as I could see, it was very thriving, and very international city.
It had lots of culture.
And it was nice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it turned sour when Hitler really started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't nice anymore.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Wait a minute.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the neighborhood of the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN], I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For-- we-- we moved around quite a bit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know for what reason.
But we did.
I remember 1, 2, 3 different places.
Ordinary apartments.
And at one point, the last one, my mother had to-- the rents were very expensive.
And she didn't have the income like that anymore.
So she rented out two or three rooms.
Besides, it was also a fact that Jews were not allowed to have an apartment for themselves.
They had to share.
[SIGHS] I think it was in school, from one of my teachers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember.
But I think that was it.
It was a known-- known fact.
I mean, you-- you-- it was-- it was forced antisemitism.
It was told.
People have been told the Jews are this and the Jews are that.
And you were not so much aware of being different in the beginning.
You were much more assimilated.
And suddenly, you were a different person.
It was only that one teacher.
All the others were pretty nice.
And especially our principal was a very nice man.
And I was at a point, I think, the last Jewish girl in school.
And he always try to keep me.
And he said-- I remember once he said to me, Mendel, I want you to finish.
I want you to be able to have an education.
And I will try not to report-- they had to report.
And I will try not to report as long as I can possibly do it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all what I remember.
And I remember his name.
It was Dr.
Michaelis.
I don't know.
I think that-- we did not immigrate.
That a lot of my friends and our acquaintances did immigrate.
And I would love to go out.
And I told my mother, I would like to get out of Germany too.
And she always said, nothing will happen to us.
Your father was an officer in the German army, and I'm a-- considered a war widow.
And nothing will happen to us.
And when it was really happening, it was too late.
She had no connection to get out.
She tried everything.
I know she paid money for a visa to-- to Cuba.
And this didn't work.
And then she tried to go to Chile.
And nothing.
It was too late.
Well, we were all frightened.
We saw that it's not getting better, it's getting worse.
And we were forced to-- to work in forced labor fields, in-- in factories, and in-- in all kind of institutions, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with very little or-- very, very little pay.
And then little by little, it got every-- every day worse.
You had to wear the Star.
And you had to-- we're not allowed to sit on this bench or do this.
We're not allowed to use subways or trams.
If you lived in a certain mileage from your job, you got a pass that you could use the subway or the bus at certain hours.
Otherwise, you had to walk.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's actually all I remember.
Everybody knows it got worse and worse.
And then we were replaced, even in the factories, by Russian and Polish prisoners of war.
And they didn't need us anymore, and--
To entertain myself?
We had friends.
And we met-- you mean after the-- when the Nazis started in '33?
Well, I was-- that was already after 1933.
So we had friends.
And we stuck together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we had a fairly normal life until 1937, '38. '37, yeah. '38.
We went swimming, and we went to the beach.
And we went and visited each other.
And we had little home parties.
Berlin is a city with lots of water.
There was Wannsee, the Little Wannsee, the Big Wannsee.
Oh, a lot of beaches.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I don't recall.
I remember once that I went to a theater to saw a-- and I saw At-- I think at the [GERMAN], or some kind of [GERMAN].
We had like a daily German newspaper.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't even recall what kind of papers we had or any-- I was too young for that.
I-- I don't remember.
Very limited.
Very little.
By seeing it, by experiencing it.
Well, that we were then forbidden to go into a movie.
We were forbidden to go to the beach.
We were to-- forbidden to do almost anything.
We were singled out.
Oh, there were announcements.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were in the papers, and over the radio.
And there-- there must have been a Jewish paper where all the laws were printed.
Not that I was aware of.
I mean, they-- I-- in the beginning, it was possible because you didn't have to wear the Star of David.
So you took a chance to use a subway, or to do this or-- you know, you-- you didn't get caught immediately all the time.
But once you-- I don't even remember when that was.
I think it was 1940 or '41, when you had to start wearing the yellow star.
And even then, some people didn't sew it on the coat.
They put it on with a certain way, that you could take it off and put it on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and tried to, you know, go a little bit against the law.
I did that too.
90% of the time.
Only when I wanted to sneak into a movie or meet my friends at a certain place where we were not allowed.
We did it.
Or we wore our handbag like this, that you didn't see it.
I-- I really don't remember everything about that.
Because this is somehow that you-- that you don't want to keep that.
You don't want to think about it.
If you mean if we light-- lit candles on Friday?
No, we did not.
My mother did not do that, because she felt she's alone and there's no husband.
I don't know why.
But it wasn't done.
Except that in the home where I was, we did it all the time.
And in the house by my aunt and uncle, we did it.
She knew it was a holiday, and she went to occasionally to a temple on the high holy days, but that was about it.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] I would say that 80% of my friends got killed in the Holocaust.
And the other 20% immigrated [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Israel, to South America, and to here.
I have friends still here, in the United States, in New York.
And I have one girl I'm very friendly with.
I still correspond with her.
She's a Gentile girl, in Berlin.
Or was it '41, at the end of '41?
I don't remember.
It could be in the beginning of '42.
Yes.
I have to figure it out.
I don't know when we went to school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stopped school in '38.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] So I was 15 years old.
In the-- the first or second year of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] high-- it was different in Germany.
Everything in general, and-- whatever you're being taught in school, plus languages.
English and French was our-- the language we had to learn.
The funny thing is when I went to Hebrew school, I liked Biblical history.
And I liked history altogether, and Greek mythology I liked.
I was never crazy about arithmetic.
And French was awful.
English I picked up well.
Because I had to.
I was thrown out.
Was I 15? '38, '23, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I had to-- what did I do, actually?
Nothing.
Oh, yes.
I tried to learn a trade.
I went to school and learned manicuring, and-- because we always thought we might immigrate.
And that didn't work out.
I was-- wanted to become a cosmetician.
And I was-- had an interview with the offices of Elizabeth Arden.
And they were not allowed to employ me, because I was Jewish.
And then I went through somebody, or through a friend of ours.
I don't remember.
To somebody who taught me to become a seamstress, to-- to learn sewing.
OK.
